questions.text: A logistic regression model predicts pass/fail from hours studied $x$ using weight $w = 1$ and bias $b = -3$. Compute the pre-activation $z = wx + b$ for $x = 5$.
Using the same model ($w=1$, $b=-3$, $x=5$, so $z=2$), compute the predicted probability $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ (2 decimal places).
Same model ($w = 1$, $b = -3$). Compute the pre-activation $z$ for a student who studied $x = 1$ hour.
For the student in the previous question ($z = -2$), compute $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ (2 decimal places).
For the model $w = 1$, $b = -3$, find the $x$-value of the decision boundary, i.e. solve $wx + b = 0$ for $x$.
questions.explanation: $wx + b = 0 \Rightarrow x = -b/w = -(-3)/1 = 3$. Students with $x \ge 3$ hours are predicted to pass.

questions.text: Why is ordinary linear regression a poor choice for predicting a binary pass/fail label directly?
choices: Its predictions are always whole numbers
Its output is unbounded, so it is not naturally a probability between 0 and 1
It cannot take more than one input feature
questions.explanation: A fitted line $y = wx + b$ can output any real number (e.g. $1.4$ or $-0.1$), which cannot be read as a probability. Logistic regression fixes this by squashing the weighted sum with the sigmoid.

questions.text: What does the sigmoid function $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ do to the pre-activation $z$?
choices: Squashes any real number into the open interval $(0, 1)$
questions.explanation: The sigmoid maps the unbounded pre-activation $z$ onto $(0, 1)$, which can be read as a probability that the example belongs to class 1.

questions.text: Using the standard threshold, a predicted probability of exactly $\sigma(z) = 0.5$ corresponds to…
questions.explanation: $\sigma(z) = 0.5$ exactly when $z = 0$, since $\sigma$ is a strictly increasing function crossing $0.5$ at $z=0$. This is precisely the decision boundary, where the model is maximally uncertain.

questions.text: Which of the following are true about a logistic regression decision boundary (where $\sigma(z) = 0.5$)?
choices: It is at the same location as where a step-activation perceptron with the same $w, b$ would switch outputs
It always passes through the origin $x = 0$
The model is maximally uncertain there
questions.explanation: The boundary is wherever $z = wx+b = 0$, which is generally not the origin unless $b = 0$. Since a step perceptron also switches at $z = 0$, the two models agree on the boundary's location; both are maximally uncertain (or on the firing/non-firing edge) exactly there.

questions.text: A classifier with boundary $x = 3$ (predict pass if $x \ge 3$) is tested on 8 students: actual pass/fail labels are (hours, actual) = (1,fail), (2,fail), (2.5,pass), (3,pass), (4,fail), (5,pass), (6,pass), (7,pass). Predicting from the boundary, how many True Positives (predicted pass AND actually passed) are there?
questions.explanation: Predicted pass ($x \ge 3$): students at $x=3,4,5,6,7$. Of these, actual pass is true for $x=3,5,6,7$ (four students) — $x=4$ actually failed (a false positive). So $TP = 4$.

questions.text: For the same 8-student test set ($TP=4$, $FP=1$, $FN=1$, $TN=2$), compute the precision $= \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$.
questions.explanation: $\text{precision} = \frac{4}{4+1} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8$: of the 5 students predicted to pass, 4 actually did.

questions.text: For the same 8-student test set ($TP=4$, $FP=1$, $FN=1$, $TN=2$), compute the recall $= \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$.
questions.explanation: $\text{recall} = \frac{4}{4+1} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8$: of the 5 students who actually passed, the classifier correctly caught 4.

questions.text: For the same 8-student test set ($TP=4$, $TN=2$, $FP=1$, $FN=1$), compute the accuracy $= \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$.
questions.explanation: $\text{accuracy} = \frac{4+2}{8} = \frac{6}{8} = 0.75$: the classifier is correct on 6 of the 8 students.

questions.text: What is the name of the smooth, S-shaped function $\dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ used to turn a weighted sum into a probability in logistic regression?
questions.explanation: $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ is the **logistic sigmoid**. It squashes any real number into $(0,1)$, giving logistic regression its name.
